title: Long-stitch journal over tapes
subtitle: A sewn multi-signature journal with exposed spine stitching over linen tapes
excerpt: Long-stitch binding sews each signature individually to a set of horizontal tapes or cords at the spine, with no kettle stitch linking the signatures. The sewing path runs the full length of each signature in a single long stitch, hence the name. The finished spine shows the tapes and the thread in a geometric pattern that is both structural and decorative.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on sewing on tapes and cords, the long-stitch and its variations. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cyril Davenport, The Book: Its History and Development (1907), chapter on earlier binding structures and their adaptation in the Arts-and-Crafts revival.

content.text: directly, with a single thread that runs from the head to the tail of each signature and wraps around the tape before returning. The

leaves a visible thread path on the spine between signatures: on a five-signature book with three tapes, the spine shows three vertical columns of thread wraps and two diagonal threads between each column per signature. The result is geometric and structural at the same time.

attrs.body: Change the blade before beginning and cut against the ruler with the free hand behind the cut line throughout.
attrs.title: Cutting tools

attrs.body: Use acid-free PVA for the tape ends; apply in a thin film and press immediately.
attrs.title: Bookbinding adhesives

items.qty: Three lengths, each 60 mm wider than the text block width
items.name: Linen bookbinding tape, 15 mm wide

items.qty: Approximately 3× the height of the book per signature (15 m total for 5 signatures)
items.name: Waxed linen bookbinding thread, 4-cord

items.qty: 2 pieces, A5 + 3 mm squares on three sides
items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm

items.qty: 2 pieces, 15 mm turn-in on all sides
items.name: Book cloth for covers

items.qty: Small amount for tape attachment and cover boards
items.name: Acid-free PVA
Beeswax block, bone folder, craft knife

content.text: Fold and score five signatures of four sheets each (eight pages per signature). Mark sewing stations on each signature: two end stations 8 mm from the head and tail, and three tape stations at positions 40 mm, 105 mm, and 170 mm from the head (for an A5 spine of 210 mm). Each tape station is actually two holes spaced 6 mm apart, one on each side of the tape. Pierce all holes through each signature from inside to outside.

Hold the three tapes against the spine of a spare board or a jig at the three tape positions, held under masking tape at the sides. The tapes must be taut and parallel. This is the sewing frame substitute, a proper bookbinding frame is ideal but not required for home binding at this scale.

Cut thread at three times the height of signature 1. Begin at the tail end station of signature 1: enter from outside to inside. Sew toward the head, entering and exiting at each tape station (entering before the tape on one side, looping around the tape, and exiting on the other side). At the head station, exit outside.

Attach signature 2. At the head of signature 1, the thread exits outside. Lay signature 2 immediately above signature 1. Enter signature 2 at its head end station from outside to inside. Work back toward the tail, entering and exiting at each tape station, wrapping around each tape in the same direction as on signature 1. At the tail station of signature 2, work the
before adding signature 3. Continue for signatures 3, 4, and 5.

Trim each tape to 30 mm beyond the spine edges. Cover the boards. Lay each tape flat against the inside face of the corresponding board, apply PVA under the tape end, and burnish flat. Allow to dry before closing. The tape holds each board to the text block without adhesive at the spine itself.
Press the finished journal under weights for one hour. The
can be trimmed lightly after pressing if signatures are uneven. Store upright with neighbouring books for support.

attrs.intro: Problems that arise in long-stitch binding.

items.fix: The two holes at each tape station should be 4 to 6 mm apart, not more. Re-pierce if needed and sew again from that station.
items.cause: The two holes at each tape station are too far apart, leaving the tape insufficient purchase on the thread wrap.
items.symptom: The thread at the tape stations is loose and the tapes slide under tension

items.fix: Before starting each new signature, hold the stack of sewn signatures together and tap the head edge on the table to seat them. The new signature should sit flush against the previous one before the needle enters the first station.
items.cause: Signatures were not packed snugly against each other before sewing.
items.symptom: The spine shows uneven gaps between signatures

attrs.heading: Common problems
